DIRECT DIAL NUMBER 
235 Montgomery Street 
(415) 983-1585 
HAND DELIVERED 
Ms. Jeanne Pritchard 
TELEPHONE (4IS) 983-1000 
CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS" 
TELEX 34743 
TELECOPIER (4IS) 398-2096 
December 20, 1989 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Jeanne: 
I would appreciate it if you would confirm your 
oral advice to me on December 15 that a law firm or other 
service provider which renders services to a candidate or 
committee may settle with its client for less than the total 
amount of the billed fees for services, providing such 
settlement is in the ordinary course of business, without 
being deemed to have made a political contribution or 
expenditure. 
Factors entering into such settlement would 
include an assessment of the client's ability to pay and the 
desire of the law firm to avoid delays in payment. 
Thank you for your attention on this matter. 
~A:-_4w~rk 
Frederick K. Lowell

sections.question: whether a law firm or other service provider which renders services to a candidate or committee may settle with its client for less than the total amount of the billed fees for services, providing such settlement is in the ordinary course of business, without being deemed to have made a political contribution or expenditure
sections.conclusion: If a law firm or other service provider settles fees for less than the full amount billed, the settlement would not be considered a campaign contribution when such settlement is in the ordinary course of business and based on reasonable factors such as the client's ability to pay and the desire to avoid payment delays. However, if the settlement is made to provide political benefit or if reasonable steps were not taken to collect the debt, the amount reduced would be considered a campaign contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: May a law firm or service provider settle unpaid fees for less than the full amount owed to a candidate or committee without this reduction being treated as a campaign contribution?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Fee settlements below billed amounts are generally not campaign contributions if made in the ordinary course of business based on standard collection practices, but become contributions if motivated by political benefit or inadequate collection efforts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is fragmented with OCR/scanning issues affecting readability. Incoming request letter from Frederick K. Lowell dated December 20, 1989 is included with FPPC response dated January 26, 1990. Question and conclusion sections were reconstructed from available text despite formatting problems. Some technical details and citations may be incomplete due to document quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: whether a law firm or other service provider which renders services to a candidate or committee may settle with its client for less than the total amount of the billed fees for services, providing such settlement is in the ordinary course of business, without being deemed to have made a political contribution or expenditure

CONCLUSION: If a law firm or other service provider settles fees for less than the full amount billed, the settlement would not be considered a campaign contribution when such settlement is in the ordinary course of business and based on reasonable factors such as the client's ability to pay and the desire to avoid payment delays. However, if the settlement is made to provide political benefit or if reasonable steps were not taken to collect the debt, the amount reduced would be considered a campaign contribution.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming oral advice regarding whether law firm fee settlements with candidates or committees for less than billed amounts constitute campaign contributions. The FPPC concluded such settlements are not contributions when made in the ordinary course of business with proper assessment factors, but are contributions if motivated by political considerations.
